lessons.title: Thrust-to-Weight Ratio
lessons.body: A drone needs thrust greater than its weight to fly.[cite: 2] Cinematic drones aim for 2:1 for smooth flight, but racing drones aim for 5:1 or higher for instant punch-outs![cite: 2]

lessons.title: Battery Voltage (Cells)
lessons.body: LiPo batteries are made of 'Cells' (S).[cite: 2] A 4S battery is 14.8V, while a 6S is 22.2V.[cite: 2] More voltage means motors spin faster, but your ESC must be rated to handle it![cite: 2]

lessons.title: Propeller Pitch & Size
lessons.body: Larger props generate more lift efficiently but take longer to change speed.[cite: 2] If you put 7-inch props on a 5-inch frame, they will hit each other![cite: 2]

lessons.title: The Brains: FC
lessons.body: The Flight Controller loops thousands of times a second, reading the gyroscope to keep the drone level.[cite: 2] Complex tasks like GPS rescue require faster processors like an F7.[cite: 2]

lessons.title: What is a Quadcopter?
lessons.body: A quadcopter is a special flying robot with exactly four motors. Two motors spin one way, and two spin the other way to keep it perfectly balanced!

lessons.title: Safety First!
lessons.body: Spinning propellers are like fast lawnmower blades. Always take the propellers off when plugging your drone into the computer so you don't accidentally get a boo-boo!

lessons.title: The Transmitter
lessons.body: This is your remote control! It has two joysticks that let you tell the drone to go up, down, left, right, and even do cool flips in the air.

lessons.title: How Drones Turn (Yaw)
lessons.body: To spin left or right like a top, the drone slows down two motors and speeds up the other two. It's a neat physics trick!

lessons.title: Moving Forward (Pitch)
lessons.body: To fly forward, the back motors spin faster to lift the tail up. The drone points its nose down and zooms straight ahead!

lessons.title: Moving Sideways (Roll)
lessons.body: Want to slide to the left? The right motors will spin faster, tilting the drone to the left so it slides through the air sideways.

lessons.title: Hovering in the Sky
lessons.body: When a drone hovers, the lifting push from the propellers perfectly matches the gravity pulling it down. It floats in place like magic!

lessons.title: The FPV Camera
lessons.body: This tiny camera sits at the front of the drone. It acts like the drone's eyeballs, looking forward to see where it's going.

lessons.title: FPV Goggles
lessons.body: When you put on FPV goggles, the drone sends video straight to screens in front of your eyes. It feels like you are sitting right inside the drone!

lessons.title: The VTX (Video Transmitter)
lessons.body: The VTX is like a super-fast mailman. It grabs the video from the camera and beams it invisibly through the air to your goggles.

lessons.title: Radio Waves
lessons.body: Drones talk to your remote using invisible radio waves. Think of them like invisible strings connecting your remote control to the drone in the sky.

lessons.title: Antennas
lessons.body: Antennas are the drone's ears. They need to stick out so they can clearly hear the invisible radio waves sent by your remote control.

lessons.title: The Drone Frame
lessons.body: Most drone frames are made of Carbon Fiber. It looks like cool black cloth baked into a hard plastic. It is super light but stronger than steel!

lessons.title: Drone Arms
lessons.body: The arms are the longest parts of the frame that stick out. The strong motors are screwed onto the very ends of the arms.

lessons.title: Power Distribution Board
lessons.body: A PDB is like a city's water pipe system. It takes the big electricity from the battery and shares it out to all the different computer parts.

lessons.title: Soldering Wires
lessons.body: To connect the wires on a drone, we use a hot tool to melt special metal called solder. It cools down fast and glues the wires safely together!

lessons.title: Brushless Motors
lessons.body: Drone motors are called 'brushless' because they use super strong magnets to push and pull each other, meaning they can spin incredibly fast without wearing out.

lessons.title: Stator and Rotor
lessons.body: The inside of the motor that stays still is the stator. The outside bell that spins around and holds the propeller is called the rotor.

lessons.title: Clockwise vs. Counter-Clockwise
lessons.body: Props are designed to scoop air in one direction. Two must spin clockwise (like a clock's hands) and two must spin counter-clockwise (backwards!).

lessons.title: Propeller Nuts
lessons.body: Because the motors spin thousands of times a minute, we use special twisty nuts to lock the propellers down tight so they don't fly off in the sky!

lessons.title: Taking Care of Batteries
lessons.body: LiPo batteries are like a grumpy pet. If you drain all their energy until they are completely empty, they will get damaged and won't hold a charge anymore!

lessons.title: Charging Safely
lessons.body: Always use a special smart charger for drone batteries, and keep an eye on them while they charge. Never leave them charging alone!

lessons.title: Battery Capacity (mAh)
lessons.body: Capacity is measured in mAh, which is how big the drone's gas tank is. A 1500mAh battery lets you fly longer than a 1000mAh battery.

lessons.title: C-Rating
lessons.body: The C-Rating tells you how fast the battery can pour out its energy. Racing drones need high C-Ratings to get lots of energy all at once!

lessons.title: ESC Protocols (DSHOT)
lessons.body: The brain of the drone talks to the motor controllers using a super-fast digital language called DSHOT. It’s like a secret code of ones and zeros.

lessons.title: Angle Mode (Training Wheels)
lessons.body: In Angle Mode, the drone's computer won't let it flip over. If you let go of the sticks, the drone will automatically flatten out and balance itself!

lessons.title: Acro Mode (Pro Mode!)
lessons.body: Acro means Acrobatics! In this mode, the drone does exactly what you tell it to do. If you tell it to do a backflip, it will keep flipping until you tell it to stop!

lessons.title: Turtle Mode
lessons.body: If you crash upside down in the grass, Turtle Mode lets you spin just two motors backward to flip the drone right-side up, just like a turtle flipping over!

lessons.title: OSD (On-Screen Display)
lessons.body: The OSD paints cool numbers over your video feed. It tells you your battery level and how long you've been flying, just like a video game screen.

lessons.title: GPS Satellites
lessons.body: If your drone has a GPS, it talks to satellites in outer space to figure out its exact location on Earth. Pretty futuristic!

lessons.title: Return to Home (RTH)
lessons.body: If your drone ever loses signal or you get lost, a drone with GPS can use its autopilot brain to fly all the way back to where you took off and land safely.

lessons.title: LED Lights
lessons.body: Adding bright LED strips makes your drone look like a flying spaceship! It also helps your friends see you when you are flying together.

lessons.title: The Finder Buzzer
lessons.body: A buzzer is a super loud beeper you can attach. If you crash in tall grass and can't find your drone, you can make it beep loud so you can hear it!

lessons.title: Action Cameras
lessons.body: While the FPV camera is for you to see, a tough Action Camera strapped to the top records beautiful HD video of your flight for you to show your family later!

lessons.title: ND Filters
lessons.body: An ND Filter is like a pair of cool sunglasses for your drone's camera. It makes colors look better on very sunny days.

lessons.title: Soft Mounting
lessons.body: Motors shake a lot! We use little rubber squishies called 'soft mounts' under the computer brain so the shaking doesn't give the drone a headache.

lessons.title: PID Tuning
lessons.body: PID Tuning is like teaching your drone good manners. You change numbers in the computer so it learns to fly perfectly smooth instead of wobbling.

lessons.title: Rates
lessons.body: Rates control how sensitive the remote is. Low rates mean the drone turns slowly. High rates mean it spins like a tornado when you move the sticks!

lessons.title: Flight Simulators
lessons.body: Before flying a real drone, you can plug your remote into your computer and practice in a video game! If you crash, it costs zero dollars to fix!

lessons.title: Pre-Flight Check
lessons.body: Just like a real airplane pilot, you should wiggle the propellers and check all the screws to make sure everything is tight before you fly.

lessons.title: Having a Spotter
lessons.body: When you have goggles on, you can't see the real world! A 'spotter' is a friend who stands next to you and watches out for birds or people.

lessons.title: Flying in the Wind
lessons.body: Drones are strong, but flying on super windy days can be tricky! The wind will try to push the drone away, so the motors have to work extra hard.

lessons.title: Line of Sight (LOS)
lessons.body: LOS flying is when you take off your goggles and just watch the drone with your own eyes while you fly it. It is great practice for beginners!

lessons.title: Freestyle Flying
lessons.body: Freestyle is like skateboarding or dancing in the sky. Pilots swoop through trees, do flips over buildings, and create beautiful, smooth videos.

lessons.title: Drone Racing
lessons.body: Drone racing is a super-fast sport! Pilots race on a track filled with gates and flags, trying to cross the finish line first without crashing.

lessons.title: Crashing is Learning
lessons.body: Everyone who flies a drone crashes sometimes! It's perfectly okay. Fixing broken propellers or arms is half the fun of being a drone builder!
